narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad studies and prepping for the LSAT—it's tough to hear that your application to our law program didn't make the cut, based on our model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. The model looked at your profile holistically and predicted a 51% chance of not passing the bar, tipping the scales just enough to pause here. What stood out most was your Year 1 decile of 4.0, which was the biggest signal pulling toward that risk—it suggested an early struggle to rank among the top performers in a competitive school environment, something the model sees as a strong predictor of later hurdles. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 34 carried significant weight in the same direction, as it fell short of the benchmarks where others with higher scores tend to thrive through law school and beyond. Your Year 3 decile of 1.0 amplified this pattern, painting a picture of grades that trended downward over time rather than building momentum. Even with your solid upper-middle family income, which might offer some support, it didn't counterbalance these academic signals in the model's view—in fact, it nudged slightly toward caution. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 added to that cautious story, as did factors like your white racial background and full-time study plans, which the model associates with certain risk patterns from past students. Your gender as female was a smaller piece but still contributed to the overall lean toward failure odds. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances, but this combination told the model a story of a challenging academic trajectory that too often leads to bar struggles. That said, law school isn't the only path, and with targeted steps—like bolstering those early grading habits or retaking the LSAT—you could rewrite this story elsewhere. We're rooting for you to find the right fit and succeed.
